description.summary: Kunekune is an internet-born Japanese urban legend describing a slender, white humanoid figure said to appear at midday in summer rice fields, writhing in the wind. Looking at it closely is said to drive the observer insane.
description.full: Kunekune (くねくね, 'wiggling') is an internet-born Japanese urban legend that emerged on the message-board site 2channel in 2001. The figure is described as a slender, white, humanoid shape resembling a paper mannequin or a piece of fine fabric, observed at lunchtime on hot summer days in widely extended rice fields and, more rarely, over the open sea. Its limbs writhe constantly, as if there were a steady wind, even when the air is still. The Kunekune is said to be safe when observed only from a distance; according to the legend, a viewer who tries to make out its features close-up will go insane, and in some variants anyone who actually approaches or touches it will die. The story originated in a short first-person 'eyewitness' horror text posted on 2channel in 2001 and was elaborated in the same template by other users; mundane explanations have included scarecrows, plastic 'wick drains' used in agriculture, and heat-stroke or dehydration hallucinations.

etymology.origin: Japanese onomatopoeia くねくね (kunekune), describing a wiggling or writhing motion.
etymology.meaning: 'Wiggling' or 'writhing'; named for the figure's reported limb movements.
etymology.first_use: First documented use as the name of the entity in a 2channel post in 2001.

related_phenomena.name: Slender Man
related_phenomena.description: American internet-born tall thin humanoid creepypasta

related_phenomena.name: Hachishakusama
related_phenomena.description: Japanese internet-born tall woman urban legend

features.description: Tall, thin, white shape resembling a paper mannequin or piece of fine fabric, with a distinctly humanoid outline
Arms and body wiggle continuously as if in wind, even on still days
Variants insist that the figure has no resolvable features at distance and that closer observation is precisely what causes harm

physical.morphology: Described in narrative rather than naturalistic terms: a flat, fabric-like or paper-like humanoid form whose movement is its defining trait.

abilities.description: Looking at the Kunekune through binoculars or otherwise resolving its details at distance is said to cause madness or catatonia in the observer
In some variants, anyone who approaches or touches the Kunekune dies
Said to be visible only at a distance and to ignore observers who do not approach

patterns: Appears at midday in hot summer weather
Stays in the middle of open fields
Harms only those who try to see it clearly or approach it

range.description: A Japanese internet-born legend; locations are narrative rather than mapped, but the canonical setting is the rural Japanese rice paddy.

peak_activity_periods.description: Peak of the original 2channel-driven Japanese internet-folklore wave; many derivative 'eyewitness' accounts.
Translation into English-language creepypasta circles and inclusion in lists of notable Japanese internet legends.

temporal.seasonal_patterns: The canonical sighting setting is midday during hot Japanese summers.

origins.cultural_roots: Born on the Japanese imageboard / message-board ecosystem (2channel) of the early 2000s, drawing on rural Japanese landscape imagery (the rice paddy, the cicada-loud summer afternoon) and on older folkloric anxieties about misperception in heat-shimmered open fields.
origins.earliest_accounts: First-person horror post on 2channel in 2001 describing the writer and a sibling sighting a wiggling white figure across a summer rice paddy.
origins.folklore_connections: Compared to other internet-born entities (Slender Man, Hachishakusama) and to traditional Japanese landscape ghosts. Skeptical writers compare the figure to scarecrows, plastic agricultural wick drains, and to fata-morgana / heat-haze illusions in open fields.

timeline.event: First Kunekune story posted on 2channel, describing a sibling sighting in a rural rice field
timeline.significance: Origin of the Kunekune urban legend

timeline.event: Spread of derivative 'eyewitness' Kunekune accounts on 2channel through summer 2001

history.modern_era: Kunekune is a fixture of Japanese internet horror and a recurring reference point in academic and journalistic discussions of online folklore and creepypasta. The figure has been compared to Slender Man both in its memetic spread pattern and in its distance-only-visible logic.

documentary.name: Original 2channel Kunekune post and follow-ups
documentary.description: The 2channel posts that introduced and propagated the Kunekune as an internet legend.
documentary.analysis: Constitutes the documented origin of the legend; the artefact is the textual thread itself, not any external evidence of a creature.

paranormal.name: Folkloric / supernatural reading
paranormal.description: The Kunekune is treated within the legend as a genuinely supernatural entity whose nature exceeds resolution by close observation, in keeping with the broader Japanese yōkai tradition of beings whose harm is tied to the act of perceiving them.
evidence_cited: The thematic continuity with traditional Japanese 'do not look' folklore tropes

scientific.name: Misidentification of agricultural objects
scientific.description: Sightings are explained as scarecrows, white plastic 'wick drain' tubes used in soft-soil agricultural drainage, or windsock-like markers in fields.
evidence_cited: Visual similarity of these objects to a wiggling humanoid form at distance

scientific.name: Heat-haze and physiological explanations
scientific.description: Reported sightings are attributed to mirages over hot rice paddies, dense midday fog, and to perceptual distortions caused by heat stroke and dehydration.
evidence_cited: Documented mirage and heat-shimmer effects over flooded paddies in midsummer

skeptical.name: Internet-born urban legend
skeptical.description: The Kunekune is, on its own face, an artefact of online storytelling: an explicitly fictional 'creepypasta' template established on 2channel in 2001, propagated through first-person 'eyewitness' posts written to that template.
evidence_cited: Pattern of memetic spread identical to Slender Man and similar online entities

stories: The original 2channel post about siblings observing a wiggling white figure across a rural rice paddy
folklore.beliefs: Not a live folk-religious belief; treated as a horror-narrative tradition. The Kunekune is, however, sometimes invoked as a cautionary 'do not look closely' story to children in rural Japan.

literature: Numerous Japanese internet horror anthologies referencing the Kunekune as a canonical example of net-kaidan
other: Frequent reference point in English-language creepypasta and online-folklore writing

research.ongoing_studies: No formal research; treated by folklorists as a key example of early-21st-century internet-born urban legend.
